kept.text:
Bitcoin just took back $80,000.

Most people will call this a breakout.

They’re wrong.

This isn’t price discovery…

it’s supply getting stripped from the market.

There’s less Bitcoin available now
than there was at $70K…
and even less than at $60K.

Every move higher is happening
with fewer coins left to buy.

This is how repricing begins.

Quiet… then violent.

kept.text:
🚨 SHOCKING DISCOVERY IN ASSAM 🚨

A live WWII-era bomb found buried in Assam’s Ledo-Lekhapani region has sent shockwaves across the area! 💣
What’s even more intense? 

This wasn’t just some rusted scrap — it was a fully unexploded ordnance (UXO), still dangerous after decades. One wrong move could’ve caused a massive disaster.

Thankfully, the Indian Army’s Bomb Disposal Squad stepped in like absolute pros 🇮🇳

They quickly secured the area, evacuated locals, and neutralized the bomb safely — preventing what could have been a tragedy.

Decades later, the scars of war still haunt us… but so does the courage of those who protect us.
Salute to our heroes 👏🔥

Scientists in London have created a new reactive aluminum compound that can perform chemistry usually done with rare metals.

The material is far cheaper than platinum and palladium and can drive key industrial reactions.

Researchers say it could reduce costs and unlock new types of chemical manufacturing.

Source: NewsForce

kept.text:
“Two days before the world changed, Honor Elizabeth Wainio was in Paris.
Friends called her Lizz. She was 27, a rising district manager for Discovery Channel Stores, just home from a long-awaited European trip. She had danced at a friend's wedding in Florence. She had walked the Champs-Élysées. She had slipped into a quiet church and lit a candle for her grandmother. Years earlier, she had told her mother that if she ever got to see Paris, she could die happy.
On the morning of September 11, 2001, she boarded United Flight 93 in Newark, headed to a company meeting in San Francisco. Forty souls were on that plane.
At 9:28 a.m., four hijackers seized the cockpit. Passengers were herded to the back. Through Airfones and cell calls, they began learning what had already happened in New York — and they understood, with terrible clarity, what their flight was meant to become.
At 9:53 a.m., Lizz called her stepmother, Esther Heymann.
Her voice was steady. Not panicked. Not broken. She had reached for the phone not to be comforted, but to comfort. "It just hurts me most," she said, "that this is going to be so much harder for you than it is for me."
They breathed together over the line. They prayed. And then Lizz said something her family has carried for twenty-five years. She told Esther: "I'm going to be with Grandma." Her grandmother had lived in rural Pennsylvania — not far, as it turned out, from a quiet field outside Shanksville. Lizz could not have known that. She only knew where her heart was pointing.
Around her, passengers had taken a vote. They had decided to fight.
Her last words came as the noise of struggle reached the back of the cabin: "I have to go. They're breaking into the cockpit. I love you."
Minutes later, Flight 93 came down in that Pennsylvania field — its target, almost certainly the U.S. Capitol, never reached. Forty ordinary people had refused to be passengers in their own ending.
Today, on the hill above the crash site, stands the Tower of Voices — forty wind chimes, each tuned to its own note. When the wind comes through, they don't sound the same. They sound like individuals.
Her father still listens for one particular chime.
What would you say, in four minutes, to the people you love most?”
